data.title: Scholastic affirms 2%–4% revenue growth guidance while advancing digital IP expansion and real estate monetization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Scholastic Corporation (SCHL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Peter Warwick, President, CEO & Director, stated that "Scholastic had a productive summer, as we prepared for the back-to-school season and advanced important initiatives. As expected, our first quarter reflected the normal seasonality of our business, with an operating loss in line with previous years." He emphasized significant investor interest in the company's SoHo headquarters and Jefferson City distribution center as part of the ongoing real estate monetization process, noting, "We remain on track, with the time line, we outlined in July."
  • Warwick highlighted the company's ongoing push for financial discipline and operational leverage, while affirming full year guidance and focusing on profitable growth. He referenced strong trade sales in the Children's Book Publishing and Distribution segment, reporting that "Suzanne Collins: Sunrise on the reaping, has now sold 3.7 million copies worldwide, since its March release," and previewed several major upcoming releases, including new titles in the I Survived and Dog Man series.
  • Warwick detailed the integration of trade, fairs, and clubs into the new Children's Book group, targeting cost savings and streamlined operations. He further announced the launch of the first-ever Scholastic branded streaming app with Future Today and noted, "We expect to surpass 10 million monthly views by calendar year-end, of classic Clifford content on YouTube."
  • In Scholastic Education, Warwick acknowledged sales pressure due to "a volatile funding environment, reflecting the delay of some federal education grants and cancellation of others," but affirmed confidence in the segment's long-term potential under new leadership and a rationalized product focus.
  • Haji Glover, CFO & Executive Vice President, stated, "Revenues decreased 5% to $225.6 million. Our seasonally adjusted operating loss improved to $81.9 million from $85.6 million, in the prior year period. Reflecting cost-saving initiatives, adjusted EBITDA was a loss of $55.7 million, an improvement from a loss of $60.5 million a year ago. Net loss was $63.3 million compared to $60.3 million, in the prior year period." Glover also noted, "Book fair revenue were $34.1 million in the quarter, an increase of 18%, driven by higher Scholastic dollar redemptions."

Outlook

  • The company is "affirming our fiscal year 2026 guidance for revenue growth of 2% to 4%. Adjusted EBITDA of $160 million to $170 million and full year free cash flow between $30 million and $40 million." Management cited expectations for a strong second quarter, supported by major trade releases, and ongoing cost optimization, including an expected $10 million in incremental tariff expenses this year.
  • Warwick stated, "We remain confident in our ability to deliver meaningful profit growth, while continuing to create long-term value for our shareholders and lasting impact for children worldwide."

Financial Results

  • Glover reported, "In Children's Book Publishing and Distribution, revenues for the first quarter increased 4% to $109.4 million, reflecting growth in school book fairs. Segment adjusted operating loss improved to $34.3 million from $36.6 million in the prior year period."
  • Book Clubs revenue was $1.8 million compared to $2.7 million a year ago, attributed to mailing timing. Trade Publishing revenue was $73.5 million, flat year-over-year, with strong demand for popular franchises. Scholastic Education segment revenues were $40.1 million versus $55.7 million in the prior year, with segment adjusted operating loss at $21.2 million compared to a loss of $17 million previously. Entertainment segment revenues fell to $13.6 million from $16.6 million, with a segment adjusted operating loss of $4 million.
  • International segment revenues were $59.4 million, up from $56.8 million, with segment adjusted operating results improving to a loss of $4.1 million from $8.3 million. Unallocated overhead costs decreased by $6.6 million to $18.3 million, mainly due to employee expense reductions.
  • Seasonal net cash used by operating activities was $81.8 million compared to $41.9 million, with free cash used at $100.2 million versus $68.7 million in the prior year. Borrowings stood at $325 million and net debt at $242.8 million.

Q&A

  • Brendan Michael McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC, inquired about early feedback from new Education Solutions products and their market resonance. Jeffrey Mathews responded that "we were getting great feedback from customers around some of the new products... we are very encouraged by the -- what we're hearing particularly with knowledge library and as well as our core products, our classroom libraries and our classroom magazines."
  • McCarthy asked about variables influencing a turnaround in education spending. Mathews explained that "as there's greater funding certainty and we've seen that some of the federal programs that had been paused or some federal grants have been paused were released in late August. As there becomes more certainty, we expect that school district and -- school and district leaders will be more forthcoming."
  • McCarthy queried about the Entertainment segment's digital strategy. Warwick responded, "The digital model that we now have and the digital income that we're getting is high margin and it's going to grow... we will see the major benefits, going progressively out into the future."
  • On monetizing digital content, Glover stated, "we're really in early stages of this... we're only on 2 platforms with the opportunity to increase that to another 6 or 7 platforms... being able to actually quantify this impact is probably going to take us a few months."
  • Questions about cost structure were addressed by Glover: "We did -- we definitely took a really good look at it prior to actually given our guidance, and our guidance reflects the majority of our spend reductions. I think we announced somewhere between $15 million and $20 million of price cost reductions."
  • On guidance sensitivity, Glover highlighted potential retail market variability and tariff costs: "...the most important thing for us is the concerns of tariffs, as it reflects our business because we are a retail business. And those expenses, which we've already planned for, which is about $10 million this year, we're continuing to monitor all the things that are going on with the government, down in D.C."
  • An unknown analyst asked about the Education Solutions trajectory, and Mathews said, "we are expecting, based on the current patterns that this year will be more back-end loaded than previously... we also expect that... those headwinds will moderate over the fall and into the spring."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions focused on cost control, education funding uncertainty, and the pace of digital monetization, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone reflecting industry headwinds and timing uncertainties.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, particularly regarding digital strategy and cost savings, using phrases such as "we are pretty confident" and "I'm extremely excited and confident about where we are."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, with continued optimism about cost reductions and strategic initiatives. Analysts' tone did not shift significantly, with ongoing focus on structural challenges and forward-looking risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 was affirmed at 2% to 4% revenue growth, $160 million to $170 million in adjusted EBITDA, and $30 million to $40 million in free cash flow, unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus advanced on digital content and IP monetization, with launches like the Scholastic streaming app and greater YouTube integration, compared to a prior focus on organizational restructuring and integration.
  • Cost reduction efforts continued as a major theme, with the current quarter emphasizing realized savings and ongoing SG&A optimization. Analysts' questions persisted around education funding and digital content profitability, mirroring concerns raised previously.
  • Key metrics such as operating loss and cash usage remained aligned with historical seasonality, and management's confidence in achieving guidance was reiterated.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a "volatile funding environment" and delays in federal education grants as near-term headwinds for the Education segment.
  • Ongoing tariff expenses were estimated at $10 million for the fiscal year, and management indicated active monitoring of trade policy developments.
  • Cash flow was pressured by higher inventory purchases, timing of expense payments, and increased interest, though mitigated by cost-saving initiatives and share buybacks.
  • Uncertainty in retail and school spending, as well as the pace of digital revenue recognition, were noted as potential variables impacting guidance.

Final Takeaway

Scholastic began fiscal 2026 with continued progress on cost discipline, IP-driven digital expansion, and real estate monetization initiatives, while affirming its full-year targets for revenue growth, profitability, and free cash flow. Management emphasized confidence in the company's back-to-school momentum, robust publishing pipeline, and ongoing strategic transformation to drive long-term value for shareholders and educational communities alike.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon seeks Trump administration's help in thwarting European Union climate law
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) called Thrusday for European leaders to repeal a new climate law that would fine corporations, with CEO Darren Woods saying the directive's penalties of as much as 5% of a company's global revenue would be "bone-crushing."

The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive, which requires companies to fix human rights and environmental issues within their supply chains or face large fines and was adopted last year, is "the worst piece of legislation I've seen since I've been in this job," Woods told Bloomberg in an interview. "Given the perspective I have around the world, that says quite a bit."

In response to criticism that the law risks harming the continent's competitiveness, the European Commission proposed a set of changes earlier this year to loosen the requirements, but Woods said it would not be enough, calling for the law to be revoked entirely.

The directive could require all large businesses in Europe to develop legally binding plans to comply with the Paris Agreement climate treaty, and "opens up standing for any activist or NGO who wants to challenge those plans and take us to court," the CEO said.

Woods said he has spoken about the regulation with President Trump and other members of the administration, which has raised concerns about the directive as part of trade negotiations with the block.

Exxon (NYSE:XOM) has been "slowly been pulling out of Europe," Woods said, noting the company has shut down or exited 19 operations because of what he said was red tape that hurts business.

data.title: Research Solutions outlines $30M ARR target and AI-driven strategy through headless platform expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Research Solutions, Inc. (RSSS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Roy Olivier, President and CEO, shared that "we set many new records for the company's performance, including $21 million in ARR," highlighted a 20% ARR growth for FY '25, and reiterated focus on a $30 million platform ARR target by the end of FY '27, characterizing it as a "Big Hairy Audacious Goal" rather than formal guidance. Management emphasized a strong acquisition pipeline and the need to execute on product, marketing, and strategy fronts, particularly as AI rapidly transforms research workflows.
  • Olivier noted, "our AI-based products are organically growing at almost 4x the pace of our legacy products today," and anticipated strong AI tailwinds in the coming quarters. He stated, "We have been a company in transition from a transaction-based company into a vertical SaaS company for many years," and discussed a pivot toward a headless, API-first platform strategy designed to integrate with large language models (LLMs).
  • Olivier reported, "we brought a new Chief Revenue Officer in November of 2024 and have seen strong B2B sales in the second half of the year," leading to more large contracts and strong academic-focused sales team results.
  • William Nurthen, CFO, stated, "The fourth quarter was another really strong quarter for our business and served as further validation of how our ongoing shift to SaaS revenue is translating into expanding margins, profitability and cash flow." Nurthen highlighted $12.4 million in total Q4 revenue, $5.2 million in platform subscription revenue (up 21% year-over-year), and platform revenue as 42% of total revenue for the first time. He also reported, "We ended the quarter with $20.9 million in annual recurring revenue, or ARR, up 20% year-over-year," and set a new company record in net B2B ARR growth.
  • Nurthen added, "Gross margin for the fourth quarter was 51% a 450 basis point improvement over the fourth quarter of 2024. This was the first time in the company's history that blended gross margin has been in excess of 50% for a quarter."
  • Chief Strategy Officer Josh Nicholson described the company's API-first, headless strategy, stating, "We see site and article galaxy increasingly being used as an API-first platform. Our customers are no longer just logging into a single interface. They are embedding site directly into their own systems, dashboards and even generative AI assistance."

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit formal financial guidance but restated a focus on surpassing the $30 million platform ARR target by the end of FY '27 and expects "continued progress toward the 40 number" in the weighted rule of 40 for FY '26.
  • Nurthen explained, "I expect sales and marketing expense to look more like what we saw in the third quarter of 2025 as we look ahead to future quarters," and forecasted transaction revenue growth to remain challenging in the first half of fiscal 2026, with a potential return to growth in the back half.
  • Nurthen also projected, "From an adjusted EBITDA perspective, I expect to follow the same seasonality as last year with the first quarter being potentially a slight dip sequentially from this quarter, but a beat to last year's Q1 result. Q2 will likely be our weakest quarter and then our strongest quarters will be in the back half of the year."

Financial Results

  • Total Q4 revenue was $12.4 million, with platform subscription revenue at $5.2 million and transaction revenue at $7.3 million. Platform revenue made up 42% of total revenue, up from 35% year-over-year.
  • Annual recurring revenue at quarter-end was $20.9 million, a 20% increase year-over-year, with net B2B ARR growth at $724,000. Net total incremental ARR growth was $567,000.
  • Gross margin for Q4 was 51%, with platform business gross margin at 88.5%. Transaction business gross margin was 24.1%.
  • Operating expenses were $5.1 million. Other income was $1.2 million, attributable to a favorable earnout adjustment. Net income for Q4 was $2.4 million or $0.07 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $1.6 million, a 13% margin.
  • Full-year revenue was $49.1 million, platform subscription revenue $19 million, and transaction revenue $30.1 million. Full-year gross margin was 49.3%. Net income for fiscal 2025 was $1.3 million or $0.04 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA for the year was $5.3 million.
  • Cash flow from operations in fiscal 2025 was over $7 million, ending the year with a $12.2 million cash balance and no outstanding borrowings.

Q&A

  • Jacob Stephan, Lake Street Capital Markets, asked about the sequential uptick in ASP and drivers. Nurthen responded, "we are actually getting larger deals... we've seen some of the larger deals in our company's history."
  • Stephan asked about Resolute's integration within the API-first strategy. Olivier stated, "Resolute works much better in this headless strategy than it works as a product unless we go in and rewrite big parts of the product."
  • Stephan inquired about competitive environment in API strategy. Nicholson replied, "I think publishers will have somewhat of a challenge becoming a pan-publisher source... we're already driving them revenue."
  • Richard Baldry, ROTH Capital Partners, asked about trends in platform COGS and gross margin. Nurthen said, "we sort of stabilized the labor base... we've really tried where we could to lower or limit the increase in the hosting cost."
  • Baldry asked about sustaining AI-related deal growth rates. Nicholson explained, "as these tools ramp up, it's less looking at here's a 100-person seat license versus here's a company-wide integration... I think that will command larger check sizes at B2B."
  • Baldry probed on large deal strength. Olivier credited the new CRO, sales execution, and pricing changes, stating, "the products are being priced accordingly."
  • Derek Greenberg, Maxim Group, asked about the LibKey partnership. Olivier described it as "an opportunity to expand our academic business."
  • Greenberg asked about Site and Article Galaxy cross-sell penetration. Nurthen stated it remains "low to mid-single-digit penetration on the Article Galaxy customer base."
  • Greenberg inquired on EBITDA margin outlook. Nurthen projected maintaining above 10%, potentially between "10% to 15% range," with gross margin expected to stay above 50%.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on competitive differentiation, margin sustainability, API strategy, large deal pipeline, and new partnerships, probing for clarity and expressing cautious optimism.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing progress, momentum, and opportunities, using phrases such as "I think we're uniquely positioned" and "we remain excited about where we are."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed increased confidence, with more emphasis on operational execution and market positioning in the evolving AI landscape.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • ARR growth remained robust, though net incremental ARR was lower than the previous quarter's record. Platform revenue mix continued to increase, and gross margin surpassed 50% for the first time.
  • Management's tone shifted to a more strategic focus on AI integration and the headless API-first approach, compared to the previous quarter's emphasis on sales and marketing investments.
  • Analysts shifted from questions about operational structure and internal AI usage to inquiries about deal sizes, competitive differentiation, and API strategy.
  • Guidance language evolved from general growth expectations to specific targets and strategic milestones, including the $30 million ARR objective and the headless strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged continued declines in transaction revenue, especially in the B2C segment, citing "competitive pressures" and "seasonally a difficult time for that product."
  • Management noted, "transaction revenue growth was challenging in the back half of fiscal 2025. We expect it to continue to be challenging in the first half of fiscal year 2026."
  • The company cited the risk that AI is "shifting demand from article retrieval to structured reasoning," impacting transaction revenues.
  • Operating expenses were carefully monitored, with planned continued investments in sales, marketing, and product development, while aiming to reduce general and administrative costs.

Final Takeaway

Management at Research Solutions, Inc. highlighted a record year of ARR and profitability, driven by their ongoing transition to a SaaS and AI-driven business model. Their focus on headless, API-first platforms and a growing acquisition pipeline positions the company to pursue ambitious growth targets. While transaction revenue headwinds persist, strong cash flow and gross margin performance support continued investment in technology and sales, with management confident in capturing emerging opportunities in the rapidly evolving AI research ecosystem.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil prices drift lower as soft U.S. fuel demand offsets persistent supply concerns
data.description:
Crude oil futures added to losses as traders shifted focus from rate-cut enthusiasm to demand concerns, worried about the U.S. economic outlook a day after the Federal Reserve reduced interest rates for the first time this year.

The U.S. government reported single-family home building plunged to its lowest in nearly two-and-a-half years in August due to a glut of unsold new houses.

Meanwhile, crude oil inventories fell by 9.3M barrels last week, driven a massive drop in net crude imports, while distillate fuel stocks jumped by 4M barrels and were 8% below the five-year average for the time of year, raising concerns over the U.S. demand outlook.

The market has been waiting to see what additional sanctions the European Union might impose on Russia, but the large U.S. crude inventory draw, the Fed's rate cut, and ongoing concerns about higher OPEC+ production have "helped to keep the losses in check," ING strategists Ewa Manthey and Warren Patterson said in a note.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for October delivery finished -0.7% to $63.57/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for November delivery also setled -0.7% to $67.44/bbl.

Front-month Nymex October natural gas (NG1:COM) closed -5.2% to $2.939/MMBtu, its largest one-day dollar and percentage decline since July 23, after the Energy Information Administration reported an above-consensus 90B cf increase in underground storage, extending the inventory surplus over the five-year average to more than 200B cf.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (NYSEARCA:XLE)

data.title: Shippers including Frontline cite 'grave concerns' over U.N. ship fuel emissions deal
data.description:
A group of prominent shipping companies including Frontline (NYSE:FRO) jointly expressed "grave concerns" Thursday about the Net Zero Framework proposed for adoption next month at the United Nations' International Maritime Organization environmental committee that seeks to cut marine fuel emissions.

"We do not believe the IMO NZF will serve effectively in support of decarbonizing the maritime industry... nor ensure a level-playing field as intended," the companies told Reuters in a joint statement. "We believe that critical amendments to the IMO NZF are needed, including the consideration of realistic trajectories... before adoption can be considered."

The proposed regulation would seek to speed up decarbonization through a bigger regulatory framework, but the Trump administration opposes the agreement, and has threatened tariffs, visa restrictions and port levies on countries that support the deal.

Greek Shipping Minister Vassilis Kikilias told IMO Secretary General Arsenio Dominguez at London International Shipping Week earlier this week that improvements were needed, Reuters reported.

Separately, freight rates for Very Large Crude Carriers soared to their highest in more than two years, Reuters reported, as tanker supply tightens with a rise in Middle East exports and more arbitrage supplies to Asia.

The key VLCC spot rate on the Middle East to China route, known as TD3C , jumped to W108 on the Worldscale industry measure, the highest level since November 2022, based on data compiled by LSEG, equivalent to at least $6.6M.

"The main drivers behind the surge in September has been the open arbitrage for U.S. Gulf to East Asia flows and the subsequent tightness created by vessels committing to these very long-haul voyages," Sentosa Shipbrokers told Reuters.

Potentially relevant companies include Frontline (NYSE:FRO), Teekay (TK), Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN), CMB.Tech (CMBT), Navios Maritime Partners (NMM), Scorpio Tankers (STNG), DHT Holdings (DHT), International Seaways (INSW), SFL Corp. (SFL), Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKAF).

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:BOAT), (NYSEARCA:BWET)

data.title: Fathom Holdings announces offering of common stock; shares down 8%
data.description:
• Fathom Holdings (NASDAQ:FTHM) announced on Thursday a proposed primary offering of its common stock in an underwritten public offering.
  • The company also expects to grant to the underwriters of the offering a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 15% of the shares of common stock offered in the underwritten public offering on the same terms and conditions.
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds from the offering for general corporate purposes.  
  • The company said Roth Capital Partners is acting as sole manager for the offering.
  • FTHM -7.92% after hours to $2.2099.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: ServiceTitan to acquire Conduit Tech, enhancing HVAC sales with LiDAR technology
data.description:
• ServiceTitan (NASDAQ:TTAN) on Thursday said it has reached a definitive agreement to acquire Conduit Tech, an HVAC design and sales platform using LiDAR technology.

  • The acquisition will integrate Conduit’s tools with ServiceTitan’s AI automation products, enabling HVAC contractors to generate fast, accurate proposals and visualizations, improving customer engagement and sales.
  • Conduit’s LiDAR-powered platform allows technicians to create 3D models, load calculations and visualizations onsite in minutes, providing fast and accurate measurements of a home's interior and potential system designs.
  • The deal is expected to close in ServiceTitan’s fiscal third quarter of 2026.
  • TTAN +0.61% after hours to $117.88.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Gold price outlook still looks positive despite pulling back from highs, analyst says
data.description:
Gold prices fell Thursday in a round of profit-taking after the metal reached another record intraday high in the previous session following the Federal Reserve's policy move to cut rates by 25 bps while signaling two more reductions this year.

Analysts said investors interpreted the Federal Reserve’s policy outlook as less dovish than previously expected, despite the rate cut and signals of further easing.

"Investors judged the Fed's guidance less dovish than anticipated," MUFG analyst Soojin Kim said in a note. "Chair Powell highlighted tariff-driven inflation risks and stressed a 'meeting-by-meeting' approach to further cuts, sending the dollar higher."

Gold's price drop looks like a classic "buy the rumor, sell the fact" move, considering how widely the Fed's rate cut was expected, and reflects short-term profit-taking, Julius Baer's Carsten Menke wrote, adding such profit-taking is healthy and should not be mistaken as a sign of a changing fundamental backdrop.

The U.S. dollar index gained 0.5% on Thursday, making dollar-priced commodities more expensive for holders of other currencies.

Gold, which typically performs well in low interest rate environments and during periods of uncertainty, has gained 38% YTD.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for September delivery closed -1% to $3,643.70/oz, and front-month Comex September slver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished flat at $41.707/oz, the lowest settlement value for both metals in a week.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (NYSEARCA:IAU), (NYSEARCA:NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
